title: Inviting Others Handbook
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Inviting Others series Inviting Others Discerning, equipping, and sending leaders to start formation groups in their own rooms COMPANION HANDBOOK Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 A Letter to the Companion Welcome to Inviting Others. This handbook is for the team carrying the Inviting Others series at Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton. Most of you have walked Getting Started and Going Deeper with these families. Some of you are coming in fresh because the work has grown and we need elders on the team — elders in the New Testament sense, not the chronological one. The handbook is written for both of you. Inviting Others is not curriculum. It is the recognition of what the Spirit has already been doing across three prior series, and the communal sending of those He has formed and is now calling to start formation groups of their own. Three things you should know before you read further. First: not everyone who finished Going Deeper will be sent in the Going Out. The discernment phase (Wks 1–4) is a real gate. Some participants will be confirmed to lead; some will be confirmed in supporting roles; some will be counseled toward further formation before any sending. All three are honest outcomes, and the team must be willing to name the third option in front of the cohort. Acts 13 only sent two of the five named in the Antioch passage; the other three were doing real work, just not the work of being apostles in that moment. Second: every sent leader is sent under spiritual authority that is not CCA. Each calling confirmed leader has individual spiritual direction with someone outside CCA, and each new group has a “home” — a church or other spiritual hierarchy that recognizes the group, holds it accountable, and provides ongoing pastoral covering. CCA is the soil that grew the leader; CCA is not the institutional parent of the group. Section 3 of this handbook is explicit about why this matters and what it requires. Third: the goal of Inviting Others is to produce Level 2 leaders — not Level 5. The Affective Taxonomy applied to leadership (Section 4) names five levels of Companion development. Level 5 is multi generational, self replicating leadership; it takes years and the support of a leader’s own ongoing formation to reach. Inviting Others aims at Level 2 with the conditions in place for movement toward Level 3 across the leader’s first 6–12 months of leading. We are not graduating Companion mature leaders; we are setting them honestly on a long road and walking with them for the first six months of it. Walk gently. The leaders being sent this Going Out are people the team has known across three series of formation work; they are also people who are about to bear weight they have not borne before. Honor what they have given you across these months by naming honestly what they are and are not ready for. The work itself is the Spirit’s; we are the room He is using to confirm what He has already started. — John Section 1 — The Series at a Glance Title and theme “Inviting Others.” The title is purposefully active. Getting Started was an introduction; Going Deeper went deeper; Going Out is movement — from the room that formed you to the room you are being sent into. The big idea From MSFIG of IJH: “Group Level 5: Former members lead new groups that exhibit the same hearing culture, not by following a manual but by having been formed by it. The Level 5 culture is portable. It replicates itself through the people it has formed.” Inviting Others is the operationalization of this claim. The series equips people who have been formed by Getting Started + Going Deeper + Going Out to start formation groups of their own, in their own rooms, shaped by the Spirit for those specific rooms. The architecture is Acts 13: the Spirit speaks in a worshiping, fasting, submitted community; th

title: Week 1 — Welcome Back — Returning to the Sent Body
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 1 Welcome Back — Returning to the Sent Body The cohort returns. The gap is named. Going Out opens with a different posture. COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 1 of Going Out reopens the cohort after the interlude. The session’s primary work is honest landing — what the interlude revealed, who returned, who did not, and what posture the cohort carries into a series whose orientation is outward, not inward. WEEK 1 — WELCOME BACK / RETURNING TO THE SENT BODY (90 minutes) Aim. Reopen the cohort after the interlude. Surface what the interlude revealed in each member. Frame Going Out’s posture: Going Out is not a sequel to Going Deeper but a different kind of work — the body sent, oriented outward, with formation now feeding mission rather than the other way around. Re establish container conditions; re engage standing pairs (continuing Going Deeper pairs + new pairs for new continuers if any); set practices for Going Out. Anchor scripture. Acts 1:8 (you shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you shall be my witnesses); Hebrews 12:1–2 returning (the joy set before Him; the body running with endurance toward the joy). Connect focus. Sent. The body together discerning what it has been formed for and how the Spirit is leading it outward. Mode. Whole room. No cohort split tonight. The cohort returns as one body — the cohort that walked Going Deeper together is now reassembled to begin Going Out as that same body, even with adjustments. Center. Each member shares briefly the question they carried across the interlude (from H11.3 prompt 4 of Going Deeper) and one sentence about how the interlude landed. Lead Companion frames Going Out’s four block architecture and Going Out’s different posture. Standing pair re engagement / new pair formation. Brief Acts 1 teaching. Between session practice. Daily Rhythm Card practices continue (carried across the interlude). The question carried from H11.3 prompt 4 is brought to the standing pair this week for renewed processing in light of Wk 1’s framing. Each member sits with: ‘Where might I already be sent, that I had not noticed?’ IJH source. Acts 1:1–8 (the architectural anchor); Acts 13:1–3 (the body that worshipped, fasted, and was sent); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier — community structurally required for full spiritual operation, including for genuine outward witness); Hebrews 12:1–2 returning (‘the joy set before him’ read as missional rather than merely eschatological). WATCH FOR (Week 1 specific risks — read twice) The risks tonight are not primarily clinical. They are pastoral and architectural. The interlude have produced different kinds of landings in different members; tonight’s framing must hold them all without collapsing to the average. The participant who lost momentum across the interlude. The Rhythm Card practices may have lapsed; the question from H11.3 may have faded; the participant may arrive feeling behind. Receive without alarm. Going Out re roots the practices; the question can be re found. Cohort Companion follow up within the week. The participant whose gap surfaced something larger than expected (a vocational rupture, a relational crisis, a health diagnosis). Receive without making it the cohort’s focus tonight. Pastoral 1:1 within 48 hours. Going Out architecture has room for what surfaced; tonight is the landing, not the engagement. Honest non continuers from Going Deeper who unexpectedly attended Wk 1. Some non continuers may show up tonight wanting to reconsider. Welcome without pressure. The decision is theirs; the cohort makes room. If they decide to continue, registration logistics handled briefly outside the session. The cohort whose Wk 11 honest assessment land

title: Week 2 — What It Means to Be Sent
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 2 What It Means to Be Sent Acts 2 and 1 Peter 2 — the empowered body becomes outward witness COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 2 teaches the theology and architecture of the sent body, then engages the cohort in its first explicit witness exercise of Going Out. WEEK 2 — WHAT IT MEANS TO BE SENT (90 minutes) Aim. Walk the New Testament architecture of the sent body — Acts 2 (Pentecost; the Spirit empowers the gathered body for outward witness) and 1 Peter 2:9–10 (a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession). Distinguish witness from evangelism, witness from program, witness from performance. Each member begins to discern: what kind of sent body am I a member of, specifically? Anchor scripture. Acts 2:1–4, 38–41 (Pentecost; the Spirit’s arrival as empowerment for witness); 1 Peter 2:9–10 (the body’s identity as a sent people); Matthew 28:18–20 returning briefly (the great commission read in light of the Acts 2 empowerment, not separate from it). Connect focus. Sent. The architecture of being sent, before the specifics of where each member is sent (which is Wk 3’s work). Mode. Whole room. The teaching block is shared; the witness exercise (Block 5) is shared; closing is shared. Center. Acts 2 + 1 Peter 2 teaching (20 min). Then a witness exercise: each member, around the circle, briefly names one specific person (initials only) to whom they have been a witness across the past week’s practice (the H1.2 outside cohort Tell). What was the Tell; what was true about it; what surfaced. The cohort listens together. Between session practice. Daily noticing question intensifies: ‘Where this week am I already a witness, even unintentionally?’ Continue the weekly Tell outside the cohort. Standing pair check in this week: ‘What is the kind of sent body you sense the Spirit is making YOU specifically? Not where you are sent yet — what kind of witness?’ IJH source. Acts 2 (Pentecost as the body empowerment paradigm); 1 Peter 2:9–10 (the corporate identity of the sent body); proposed Vol 1 Exploration on baptism in the Holy Spirit as empowerment for witness (distinct from regeneration); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier — the body sent together amplifies witness, just as it amplifies hearing). WATCH FOR (Week 2 specific risks — read twice) The risks tonight are theological pastoral. The vocabulary of ‘witness’ carries baggage in many traditions; the architecture must hold without collapsing into either evangelism as program or witness as performance. Witness collapsed into evangelism as program. Some members may default to a ‘how to share my faith’ frame they learned elsewhere. The architecture is more specific: witness is the body’s outward expression of what the Spirit has formed in it. Programs are tools; witness is identity. Re frame gently when this collapse happens. Witness performed as proof of growth. Some members may report this week’s Tell as evidence of their formation. The architecture is the opposite — the witness IS the formation, lived; not its proof. The participant whose week’s Tell did not happen. ‘I forgot to do the Tell.’ ‘There was nobody to Tell.’ ‘I tried but it felt forced.’ All honest. Receive without shame; Going Out re engages each week. Re frame the practice: ‘The Tell is not a quota; it is a noticing. Where this week did the Spirit move you toward someone, and what happened?’ The participant whose Tell crossed boundaries (was experienced by the receiver as intrusion). The Spirit’s movement does not override relational care; receive the participant’s honest report; pastoral support if welcomed for refining the witness posture. Coercive evangelistic framing. ‘I converted my colleague.’ ‘I told my friend

title: Week 3 — Where Am I Being Sent
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 3 Where Am I Being Sent Cohort split discernment — each member articulates a specific sent context using the language of gift, shadow, calling, and witness COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 3 closes the body sent block. The cohort splits; each member articulates a specific sent context drawing on Going Deeper’s gift / shadow / calling work and the past two weeks’ witness emergence; the cohort merges to share. WEEK 3 — WHERE AM I BEING SENT (90 minutes) Aim. Each cohort member articulates a specific sent context for Going Out — a particular relational world, vocational context, or service rhythm where the Spirit is calling them to engage as a witness this season. The articulation draws on Going Deeper Wk 9 (gift and shadow), Going Deeper Wk 10 (Acts 13 calling integration where applicable), the Going Deeper H11.3 prompt 4 question, and the past two weeks of outside cohort Tells. The cohort holds the body of named contexts together as the foundation for Wks 4–9’s mission close to home and mission far blocks. Anchor scripture. Acts 13:1–3 (the body discerns and sends); 1 Corinthians 12:4–7 (varieties of gifts, varieties of service, the same Spirit, for the common good); Ephesians 2:10 (we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works which God prepared beforehand). Connect focus. Sent. The discernment of WHERE the witness is being made specific in each member’s life this season. Mode. Cohort split into junior teens / senior teens / parents (45 min each, parallel). The merge for shared circle close (15 min). Standing pairs do their work first within each cohort space. Center. Each member writes ONE sentence naming a specific sent context for Going Out — a place, a rhythm, a relational world. The standing pair walks the sentence with each other (12 min per direction). The cohort circle hears each member’s sentence around the room (15–20 min). Cohort Companion captures contributions on a flip chart for the merge. Between session practice. Daily noticing question shifts: ‘Lord, am I in the place you are sending me, or am I avoiding it?’ Continue the weekly Tell outside the cohort — ideally now from within the named sent context. Standing pair check in this week: ‘What is one specific obstacle the Spirit is showing you in your sent context?’ Wk 4 begins mission close to home. IJH source. Acts 13:1–3 (Antioch sending — the body discerns specific sending); Going Deeper Wk 9 (gift and shadow as the substrate of witness); Going Deeper Wk 10 (Acts 13 calling discernment for one Discerner becomes the architectural template here for everyone). WATCH FOR (Week 3 specific risks — read twice) Tonight is the most consequential discernment of the body sent block. The risks are around premature closure, vague sent contexts that are not actually specific, and pressure to manufacture clarity that has not actually arrived. Premature closure. A member arrives at Wk 3 having decided they are sent to X, with no openness to refinement. The cohort’s witness can refine what is heard. The Lead Companion frames at the start: ‘What you bring tonight is a working sentence, not a final declaration.’ Vague sent context. ‘I am sent to be a better Christian.’ ‘I am sent to love people.’ These are not specific enough to do work. Cohort Companion redirects gently: ‘Specific. A relational world. A vocational context. A service rhythm. Where, exactly, this season?’ Manufactured clarity. The cohort’s structure can produce social pressure to have a sentence ready when the Spirit has not yet given one. Honest absence of clarity is real data. Re frame: ‘If you do not yet have a specific sent context, your Going Out is the discernment itself. Name the question; the cohort holds it.’ S

title: Week 4 — The Daily Tells
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 4 The Daily Tells Mission close to home opens — the body looks at what is actually happening in the past three weeks of witness practice COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 4 opens the mission close to home block. The cohort has been doing weekly outside cohort Tells for three weeks now — tonight’s work is to look honestly at what is actually happening, surface the patterns, and refine the practice for the next two weeks. WEEK 4 — THE DAILY TELLS (90 minutes) Aim. Surface what the past three weeks of outside cohort Tells have actually produced. Move from one sentence cohort circle reports (Wk 2) to substantive examination of patterns: what kinds of receivers; what kinds of moments; what kinds of cost; what kinds of fruit. The body looks at the body’s daily work and weighs what it sees. Refine the Tell practice for Wks 5–6 with specific differentiation by context. Anchor scripture. John 13:35 (‘By this all people will know you are my disciples, if you have love for one another’); 1 Peter 3:15 (‘always being prepared to make a defense… yet do it with gentleness and respect’); Colossians 4:5–6 (‘Walk in wisdom toward outsiders, making the best use of the time. Let your speech always be gracious, seasoned with salt’). Connect focus. Mission close to home. The daily relational world; the immediate sent context; people each member already knows. Mode. Whole room. The cohort’s daily Tells data is shared across cohort lines tonight; junior teens, senior teens, and parents all hear each other’s actual witness data. The cross cohort visibility is the pedagogical centre. Center. Brief teaching frame (15 min). Then a structured cohort survey: each member contributes ONE specific Tell from the past three weeks (not the most polished; the most honest), with brief data on receiver type, context, and what surfaced. Lead Companion captures patterns on flip chart in real time. Cohort weighs the patterns. Refinement for Wks 5–6 by domain (household, vocational, third place). Between session practice. The weekly outside cohort Tell continues, with a specific differentiation: this week’s Tell is in the HOUSEHOLD context (family of origin, present household, immediate domestic relationships). Daily noticing question shifts: ‘Where in my house am I a witness already, and where am I avoiding being one?’ Standing pair check in this week: ‘What is the household witness asking of you specifically?’ IJH source. Vol 1 FL.XXI Household Formation Law (the Shema’s formation mechanism); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier extended to outward witness); the ordinary life witness reading of John 13:35 and 1 Peter 3:15. WATCH FOR (Week 4 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the comparison dynamics that surface when the cohort sees each other’s actual witness data side by side. Some members will feel exposed; some will compare themselves favorably or unfavorably; some will resist the cohort level pattern surfacing. Comparison and shame. A junior teen hears a parent’s vocational Tell and feels their school friend Tell is small. A parent hears another parent’s costly family of origin Tell and feels their workplace Tell is shallow. The Lead Companion frames at the start: scale of context is not scale of faithfulness. The daily Tell to a sibling or classmate carries the same weight as a vocational Tell to a colleague. Performative reframing. A member whose Tell did not happen reports it as if it did, or describes a non Tell encounter as a Tell. The cohort’s honesty discipline has been built across Going Deeper and the first weeks of Going Out. The Lead Companion gently redirects when needed: ‘Honest data is the work. The cohort can hold a non Tell.’ The cohort that has lapsed o

title: Week 5 — The Household Witness
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 5 The Household Witness The costliest close to home context — family of origin and present household witness; standing pairs do harder work COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v2, May 2026 (household as calling configuration named) Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 5 engages the household domain — typically the costliest close to home witness context. The cohort splits; standing pairs do substantive pair work on the household witness; cohort circles surface patterns; the merge brings the body together briefly. WEEK 5 — THE HOUSEHOLD WITNESS (90 minutes) Aim. Engage the household domain — family of origin and present household — as a specific sent context for each cohort member. Surface what the past week’s household domain Tell revealed; walk the standing pair through the harder material the household surfaces; receive the cohort circle’s honest range; close briefly as a body. Anchor scripture. Deuteronomy 6:4–9 (the Shema — the household formation mechanism); Acts 16:31–34 (the Philippian jailer’s household conversion — the household as a unit of witness and formation); 1 Timothy 5:8 (‘if anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith’); 1 Peter 3:1–2 (witness without a word — specifically in marriage, but applicable broadly to long household relationships). Connect focus. Mission close to home, household domain. Where the witness is most exposed because the receivers know the witness’s history. Mode. Cohort split into junior teens / senior teens / parents (60 min each, parallel). The merge for shared circle close (10 min). Center. Brief teaching frame (10 min). Standing pair work in cohort spaces — each pair walks the household witness substantively (15 min per direction). Cohort circle hears patterns from the pair work (10 min). Cohort Companion captures patterns; the merge integrates. Between session practice. This week’s outside cohort Tell shifts to the VOCATIONAL domain (workplace, school, professional context). Daily noticing question shifts again: ‘Where in my work today is my witness being shaped, refined, or tested?’ Standing pair check in this week: ‘What is the vocational witness asking of you that the household witness made visible?’ IJH source. Vol 1 FL.XXI Household Formation Law (the Shema’s formation mechanism); Vol 1 FL.XXVIII Generational Nested Structure Law (the personal nested structure extended to household and people); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier applied to outward witness). WATCH FOR (Week 5 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks are the highest stakes pastoral risks of the body sent and mission close to home blocks. Household material surfaces decades old patterns, unresolved relational rupture, marital tension, family of origin wounds, and parenting questions. The architecture has to hold without breaking and without spreading material the cohort cannot contain. Estrangement material. A member’s household includes an estranged relationship (parent child, sibling, spouse, extended family). The week’s Tell may have surfaced what the estrangement carries. The architecture is honest engagement, not reconciliation pressure. Receive cleanly; pastoral 1:1; some estrangements are seasons; some are protective; some are unresolved decades. Marital material in the standing pair. A married participant’s household witness work surfaces marital tension. The pair partner is not the spouse; the architecture protects against detailed disclosure. Confession as architecture from Going Deeper Wk 5 holds: name the pattern, not the disclosure. Cross cohort family Tells. A teen’s household Tell involves their parent who is in another cohort, or vice versa. The cohort split tonight protects the developmental space; cro

title: Week 6 — The Vocational Witness
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 6 The Vocational Witness Workplace, school, daily rhythm contexts — where most members spend the bulk of their hours and where the three modes of witness are most visibly tested COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v2, May 2026 (vocational as calling configuration named) Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 6 closes the mission close to home block. The cohort engages the vocational domain — where most members spend the bulk of their hours, and where the three modes of witness are most visibly tested over time. WEEK 6 — THE VOCATIONAL WITNESS (90 minutes) Aim. Engage the vocational domain — workplace (parents), school (teens), professional and daily rhythm contexts — as a specific sent context for each cohort member. Surface what the past week’s vocational domain Tell revealed; explore the three modes of witness as they show up in the vocational space; close the mission close to home block by integrating household and vocational learnings; bridge to Wks 7–9 mission far. Anchor scripture. Colossians 3:23–24 (‘Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men’); Daniel 6:3–4 (Daniel’s vocational integrity — ‘no error or fault was found in him’); 1 Thessalonians 4:11–12 (aspire to live quietly, mind your own affairs, work with your hands, walk properly before outsiders); Matthew 5:16 (‘let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father’). Connect focus. Mission close to home, vocational domain. The daily rhythm context where most members spend the bulk of their hours. Mode. Whole room. The vocational domain spans cohort lines (parents have workplaces; teens have schools; both share the structure of a daily rhythm context with non believing or differently believing colleagues). Cross cohort visibility is again the pedagogical centre. Center. Brief teaching frame (12 min). Then a structured walk through the three modes as they show up vocationally: each member shares ONE specific vocational moment — not the most polished; the most revealing of how their three mode pattern operates in the daily rhythm context (35 min). Lead Companion captures patterns. Cohort weighs the patterns and the close to home block as a whole. Bridge to Wks 7–9. Between session practice. Wk 7 begins mission far. The weekly outside cohort Tell shifts to a third place or wider context domain (neighbours, hobby groups, community contacts, fellow believers in different contexts). Daily noticing question shifts: ‘Where outside my immediate world is the Spirit moving me toward someone?’ Standing pair check in this week: ‘What does mission far ask of you that mission close to home has prepared you for?’ IJH source. Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier applied to vocational presence over years); Daniel 1–6 and Genesis 39–50 (vocational integrity in alien contexts); Vol 1 FL.XXVII Thick Practice Transmission Law (how embodied practices transmit formation across long contexts — the workplace IS a long context for many). WATCH FOR (Week 6 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the chronic pressure nature of vocational witness, the comparison dynamics across vocational types, and the politicized content that workplace and school contexts often surface. Vocational type comparison. The senior teen whose school is a small Christian community hears the parent whose workplace is hostile to faith; comparison shame surfaces in either direction. The Lead Companion frames at the start: every vocational context is its own witness terrain; faithfulness is contextual. Politicized vocational content. A Tell involving political conversation, cultural debates, identity politics, or workplace DEI dynamics. The cohort’s discipline is the witness posture, not the political position. Rec

title: Week 7 — The Third-Place Network
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 7 The Third Place Network Mission far opens — neighbours, hobby groups, fellow believers in different contexts; the geography opens beyond the immediate COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v2, May 2026 (downhill/uphill distinction propagated beyond third place) Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 7 opens the mission far block. The cohort moves from the close to home work (Wks 4–6) into the broader geography — third place networks, neighbours, fellow believers in different contexts, and the wider community. The Acts 1:8 geography opens further: from Jerusalem (immediate) to Judea and Samaria (broader). WEEK 7 — THE THIRD PLACE NETWORK (90 minutes) Aim. Open the mission far block. Surface the past week’s third place / wider context Tells. Engage the cohort with the architecture of the third place network as a missional terrain. Distinguish third place witness from close to home (Wks 4–6) and from formal mission engagement (Wk 8–10). Walk Eph 2:10 (‘good works prepared beforehand’) and Acts 8 (Philip’s Samaritan mission and the Ethiopian eunuch — the Spirit moving through prepared encounters). Anchor scripture. Acts 8:4–5, 26–40 (Philip’s Samaritan mission and the Ethiopian eunuch); Ephesians 2:10 (‘we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works which God prepared beforehand’); Luke 10:1–9 (the seventy two sent ahead, into towns where Jesus was about to come). Connect focus. Mission far. The third place network as the bridging geography between close to home (vocational + household) and specific missional engagement (Wks 8–9). Mode. Whole room. The third place domain spans cohort lines and benefits from cross cohort visibility. Center. Brief teaching frame (15 min). Each member surfaces ONE third place / wider context moment from the past week (40 min around the circle, captured on flip chart). Pattern naming and integration with close to home (10 min). Bridge to Wk 8’s cohort level missional discernment (10 min). Between session practice. The weekly outside cohort Tell continues, but with a specific question added: ‘Where in my third place network might the Spirit be inviting more sustained engagement — not a single Tell, but a longer arc?’ Daily noticing question shifts: ‘Lord, what good work have you prepared, that I have been walking past?’ Standing pair check in this week: ‘What might the Spirit be inviting our COHORT into beyond what each of us is doing alone?’ (Wk 8 prep.) IJH source. Acts 8 (Philip and the Spirit’s direction); Eph 2:10 (good works prepared beforehand — Vol 2’s Mission Connect anchor); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier extended to mission engagements); Vol 2 Exp 5’s downhill mission language (the witness that flows from who you are when you are most yourself). WATCH FOR (Week 7 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the conceptual transition from close to home to mission far, the temptation to inflate third place engagement into formal mission, and the comparison dynamics between members with rich third place networks and those without. Inflation. A member treats their third place network as the missional answer when it is actually the bridging terrain. Re frame: third place is the practice ground where the mission far posture forms; specific missional engagement (Wk 8) is the next layer. Hollow third place. A member realizes they have almost no third place network — work and family consume their hours, the rest of life is digital and isolated. Receive cleanly. The realization is itself data; not all Going Out members will have rich third place to engage. Pastoral 1:1 if welcomed. Church as third place confusion. A member treats their church community as third place, when church for them is closer to second family. Distinguish carefully: third plac

title: Week 8 — Specific Missional Engagement
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 8 Specific Missional Engagement Cohort level discernment — the body listens for what the Spirit is inviting US into, beyond what each of us is doing alone (Acts 13 architecture) COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v2, May 2026 (Landing 2 named as architectural confirmation of differentiated callings) Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 8 is the architectural apex of the Going Out series — the cohort uses the Acts 13 corporate listening architecture (Going Deeper Wk 10) to discern what the Spirit may be inviting THE BODY into, beyond what each member is doing alone. The cohort prepares, listens, surfaces, weighs, and lands honestly — either a specific shared engagement, or honest data that there isn’t one this season. WEEK 8 — SPECIFIC MISSIONAL ENGAGEMENT (90 minutes) Aim. Walk the Acts 13 corporate listening architecture as a cohort. Discern what (if anything) the Spirit is inviting the cohort — as a body — into beyond what each member is doing individually. The discipline is the same as Going Deeper Wk 10’s discernment for one Discerner; tonight’s discerner is the cohort itself, with each member contributing what surfaced in the standing pair preparation conversation. Anchor scripture. Acts 13:1–3 (the Antioch sending paradigm); 1 Corinthians 14:29 (‘let two or three prophets speak, and let the others weigh what is said’); Acts 15:28 (‘it has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us’ — the Jerusalem council as the body’s discernment of corporate direction). Connect focus. Mission far. Cohort level discernment of corporate engagement. Mode. Whole room. The cohort sits as one body — the unit of discernment IS the cohort, not the individual. The Lead Companion holds the architecture; each member contributes; the body weighs together. Center. Brief teaching frame (10 min). 60 second silent settling and corporate prayer. Each member shares around the circle what surfaced in the past week’s standing pair check in (‘What might the Spirit be inviting our COHORT into beyond what each of us is doing alone?’) — 60 sec each, captured on flip chart (35 min). The cohort weighs what was heard — patterns, convergences, divergences (15 min). The body lands honestly: a specific shared engagement, or no shared engagement this season, or a clarifying question for further discernment (15 min). Between session practice. Whatever the cohort discerned tonight is integrated through the week. If a specific shared engagement was named: each member sits with it; the standing pair walks the cost of yes; the body re engages at Wk 9. If no shared engagement: each member returns to individual sent context, with the cohort’s honest discernment as the foundation. Daily noticing question: ‘What is the body of cohort witnesses asking of me this week?’ Standing pair check in: ‘Where do you land on what the cohort named?’ IJH source. Acts 13:1–3 (Antioch sending — the body that worshipped, fasted, and was sent, the architectural anchor); 1 Cor 14:29 (community testing of what is heard); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier); MSFIG (the five level group taxonomy — tonight is a Level 4 attempt, the body hearing for the body); Going Deeper Wk 10 (the Acts 13 discernment for one Discerner, scaled tonight to the cohort itself). WATCH FOR (Week 8 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the architectural integrity of corporate discernment. The cohort can either do real Level 4 work — the body hearing for the body — or it can drift into Level 3 (good work together but not corporate hearing) or even Level 2 (social fellowship that produces consensus without genuine listening). The Lead Companion’s architectural discipline is what holds the difference. Premature consensus. Cohort members converge quickly on a single proposal that eme

title: Week 9 — The Body Sent Beyond
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 9 The Body Sent Beyond Mission far closes — walking the cost of what was discerned in Wk 8; the body lays hands on the sending; preparation for the long obedience beyond Going Out COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 9 closes the mission far block. The architecture adapts to Wk 8’s landing: if specific shared engagement, walk the cost and lay hands on the sending; if individual sending, lay hands on each member’s personal sent context; if clarifying question, revisit with what surfaced across the week. Either way, Wk 9 prepares for the long obedience beyond the formal Going Out series. WEEK 9 — THE BODY SENT BEYOND (90 minutes) Aim. Close the mission far block. Walk the cost of what the body discerned in Wk 8. Lay hands on the sending — either the specific shared engagement (Landing 1) or each member’s individual sent context (Landing 2) or the carried clarifying question (Landing 3). Prepare for the long obedience that extends beyond Going Out’s formal close. Frame the bridge to Wks 10–12, which close Going Out as a whole. Anchor scripture. Acts 13:3–4 (‘Then after fasting and praying they laid their hands on them and sent them off. So, being sent out by the Holy Spirit’); Acts 14:21–23 (Paul and Barnabas’s pattern of strengthening disciples and appointing elders, then continuing); Hebrews 12:1–2 returning (‘let us run with endurance the race that is set before us’ — the long obedience after sending). Connect focus. Mission far / sending. The body sent into specific work and into the long obedience. Mode. Whole room. The cohort sits as one body for the laying on of hands. The cohort split, if it happens at all tonight, is brief and only if Landing 2 (individual sending) requires cohort specific preparation. Center. Brief teaching frame (10 min). Walk the cost of yes/no (20 min) — standing pairs do brief work, then cohort circle hears patterns. Laying on of hands sending (35 min) — the body lays hands on whatever is being sent, with brief specific blessings. Bridge to Wks 10–12 (10 min). Closing container. Between session practice. The body has now been sent. Whatever was discerned and confirmed enters integration across the week. Daily noticing question: ‘Where is the sending now showing up in concrete obedience?’ Standing pair check in: ‘What is the first specific obedience asked of you this week?’ Wk 10 begins the closing block of Going Out — sending and re formation — with honest assessment of what Going Out produced. IJH source. Acts 13:3–4 (the body sends with hands laid on); Acts 14 (the apostolic pattern of sending plus continued strengthening); Hebrews 12:1–2 (the long obedience anchor that has carried the whole IJH arc — Going Deeper opened with it; Going Out closes its mission far work referring back to it); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier through the sending; the body together produces what individual sending cannot). WATCH FOR (Week 9 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the cost counting work, the laying on of hands moment, and the integration of whatever Wk 8 actually produced. The architecture is adaptive — the team must hold the right shape for the actual landing, not the imagined one. Cost not counted. The body discerned a shared engagement in Wk 8 but tonight’s cost counting reveals that not all members can participate. Receive cleanly; the architecture protects: cohort level direction is separate from individual capacity. Each member walks honest yes/no/limited; the cohort holds what each can give. Sending pressure. A member feels coerced toward yes by the cohort’s social pressure or by the architectural moment. Receive cleanly; the discernment is theirs; the cohort’s sending is separate from individual obligation

title: Week 10 — What Going Out Produced
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 10 What Going Out Produced Individual integration — each member articulates what Going Out formed; standing pair walks each member’s trajectory; the body holds what each one carries forward COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v2, May 2026 (integration prompts honor full calling direction range) Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 10 opens Going Out’s closing block. The architecture engages what Going Out actually produced for each member — not a generic summary, the honest specifics of what was formed, what shifted, what surfaced, what remains incomplete. Wks 10–12 close the Going Out series honestly, prepare for the long obedience, and bridge to whatever continues beyond the formal series. WEEK 10 — WHAT GOING OUT PRODUCED (90 minutes) Aim. Each cohort member articulates what Going Out produced in them — specific to their working sent context sentence (Wk 3), the household and vocational engagement (Wks 5–6), the third place and missional work (Wks 7–9), and the body’s sending. The standing pair walks each member’s trajectory across the Going Out series substantively; the cohort circle hears patterns; the body holds the body of integrations as the foundation for Wk 11’s cohort level assessment. Anchor scripture. Philippians 1:6 (‘He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ’); Hebrews 12:1–2 returning (the long obedience anchor across the formation arc); 2 Timothy 4:7 (‘I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith’ — read as the long arc completion that tonight’s integration is one moment in). Connect focus. Sending and re formation. What the body has been formed into and where each member is now sent for the long obedience. Mode. Whole room. The cohort sits together for opening, teaching, and closing; the standing pair work in cohort shared space (16 min per direction); the cohort circle hears patterns briefly. Center. Brief teaching frame (10 min). Silent integration writing (10 min) using H10.1 — each member maps their Going Out trajectory across five integration domains. Standing pair walks the trajectory (32 min, 16 each direction). Cohort circle pattern surfacing (15 min). Bridge to Wk 11 cohort level assessment (10 min). Between session practice. Daily noticing question for Wk 10–11 transition: ‘What Going Out formed in me, that no other season could have formed?’ Standing pair check in this week: ‘Where do I sense the cohort as a whole landed, looking back across the Going Out series?’ (Wk 11 prep — each member arrives Wk 11 with a working assessment of where the cohort landed, so the cohort can weigh together.) IJH source. Philippians 1:6 (the work in process); Hebrews 12:1–2 (the long obedience anchor); Acts 14:21–22 and Acts 15:36 (the apostolic pattern of return and strengthen, applied to each member returning to their Going Out journey to articulate what was formed); the Affective Taxonomy (Vol 2 Exp 7) as the implicit assessment frame for individual integration; Going Deeper Wk 11 (cohort level assessment template that Wk 11 of the Going Out series scales). WATCH FOR (Week 10 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the temptation to summarize generically rather than specifically, the comparison dynamics that surface when the cohort sees each other’s Going Out trajectories, and the surfacing of unfinished work that some members need to continue carrying. Generic summary. A member articulates ‘I grew in the Going Out series; God is good’ without specifics. The architecture asks for specifics: what shifted, what surfaced, what remains. The standing pair surfaces specificity gently; the cohort circle’s pattern surfacing reveals where generic summary is masking unfinished work. Comparison shame. A member

title: Week 11 — Where the Cohort Lands as a Body
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 11 Where the Cohort Lands as a Body Cohort level honest assessment using Vol 2’s five level group taxonomy — where did the body of cohort members actually arrive, across both Going Deeper and Going Out COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 11 engages cohort level integration. The architecture parallels Going Deeper Wk 11 — honest five level group taxonomy assessment — but extended across the full year (Going Deeper + Going Out). The cohort splits to engage developmentally; the merge integrates as one body. WEEK 11 — WHERE THE COHORT LANDS AS A BODY (90 minutes) Aim. Honest cohort level assessment of where the body of cohort members landed across the full formation arc (Going Deeper plus Going Out). Use Vol 2’s five level group taxonomy as the working frame. Compare with Going Deeper Wk 11’s honest assessment from Going Deeper. Identify what the body became, what it didn’t become, and what its honest level reveals about Going Out’s formation. The honest assessment is the foundation for Wk 12’s sending into the long obedience. Anchor scripture. 1 Corinthians 12:12–27 (the body of Christ); Acts 2:42–47 (the body of the Pentecost witnesses described concretely — teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, prayers, daily life shared); Ephesians 4:11–16 (‘we are to grow up in every way into him who is the head, into Christ’). Connect focus. Sending and re formation, body level. Where the cohort lands honestly as the foundation for sending. Mode. Cohort split into junior teens / senior teens / parents (50 min each, parallel) for honest assessment within developmental peer group. The merge for shared circle integration (15 min). Center. Brief teaching frame (10 min). Cohort split honest assessment using H11.1 worksheet (30 min). Cohort circle articulates the cohort’s landing on the five level taxonomy (15 min). Merge into shared circle; Lead Companion integrates the three cohort assessments into a body level reading (15 min). Bridge to Wk 12 sending (10 min). Brief closing (5 min). Between session practice. Daily noticing question: ‘What does the cohort’s honest landing free in me, and what does it ask of me, in the long obedience after the formal series ends?’ Standing pair check in this week: ‘What blessing do I want to speak over my pair partner at Wk 12, given what we have walked together?’ (Wk 12 prep — each member arrives ready to bless their pair partner.) The team will distribute the H12.1 sending card during the week as part of pastoral 1:1s. IJH source. MSFIG (the five level group taxonomy: Social Fellowship; Information Sharing; Genuine Care; Hearing as Body; Sent as Body); 1 Cor 12 (the body); Acts 2:42–47 (the body’s concrete shared life); Going Deeper Wk 11 (the architectural template tonight scales). WATCH FOR (Week 11 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on flattering self assessment, the temptation to land at a level the cohort hopes for rather than the level the cohort actually reached, and the dynamics across the three cohorts when their honest assessments diverge. Flattering self assessment. The cohort wants to land at Level 4 or 5 — ‘we have done the formation arc; surely we are a Level 4 body.’ The architecture is honest, not flattering. A cohort that did genuine formation at Level 3 has done significant work; calling it Level 4 doesn’t make it Level 4 and undermines the honesty Going Out has built. Comparing to Going Deeper Wk 11’s assessment without honesty. Going Deeper assessment may have landed at Level 3 with Level 4 emerging; Going Out assessment should not auto promote without honest data. Sometimes Going Out confirms; sometimes it solidifies; sometimes it reveals Going Deeper’s assessment was generous. Cross cohort diverg

title: Week 12 — Sending Into the Long Obedience
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Out series Week 12 Sending Into the Long Obedience The formal series closes — standing pair blessings spoken aloud; cohort Aaronic spoken together; sending card given; bridge to whatever continues COMPANION LESSON PLAN Pilot edition — Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Curriculum draft v1, May 2026 Quick Reference Card Print this page on cardstock. Two copies in the room. Wk 12 closes the formal series. The architecture is ceremonial without being performative — standing pairs speak specific blessings aloud; the cohort speaks the Aaronic together; sending cards are given; honest bridge to whatever continues. Wk 12 carries the weight of a year’s work; the team holds the closing carefully. WEEK 12 — SENDING INTO THE LONG OBEDIENCE (90 minutes) Aim. Close the formal series honestly. Speak specific blessings between standing pair partners; the cohort speaks the Aaronic together as a body; receive the H12.1 sending card; bridge to whatever continues (Going Deeper round 2 for some; honest non continuation for others; long obedience for all). The closing is ceremonial without being performative — the body’s witnessed transformation, made specific in the blessings, sent into the long obedience. Anchor scripture. Numbers 6:24–26 (the Aaronic blessing in full); Hebrews 12:1–2 returning a final time (the long obedience anchor across the formation arc); Ephesians 4:1–3 (‘walk in a manner worthy of the calling… with all humility and gentleness, with patience, bearing with one another in love’); Acts 20:36–38 (Paul’s farewell at Miletus — weeping, kneeling, embracing, then sending; ceremonial without being performative). Connect focus. Sending and re formation, closing. The formal series ends; the long obedience continues. Mode. Whole room. The cohort sits as one body for the closing. No cohort split tonight. Center. Brief teaching frame on the architecture of closing (10 min). Standing pair blessings spoken aloud (40 min). Cohort circle: each member ONE sentence about what the formation arc produced (15 min). Cohort speaks the Aaronic together over the cohort (5 min). H12.1 sending card given (5 min). Bridge to what continues (10 min). Final closing (5 min). Between session practice. There is no between session practice for Wk 12 — the formal series ends tonight. The continued practices (Rhythm Card, weekly Tells, standing pair check ins, the H12.1 sending card’s practices) carry into the long obedience without the formal series’ weekly cohort meeting. Members continuing to Going Deeper round 2 (when offered) re engage at that point. Members not continuing carry the year’s formation into their individual contexts. The cohort’s ongoing connection to one another, beyond the formal series, is whatever each pair and each set of relationships can honestly hold. IJH source. Numbers 6:24–26 (Aaronic); Hebrews 12:1–2 (long obedience anchor across the formation arc); Acts 20:17–38 (Paul’s Miletus farewell as architectural template for the closing); Vol 2 Exp 9 (Community as Amplifier extending into the long obedience after the formal series); Vol 6 Council material on welcoming and releasing (the closing as releasing the body to continued formation, not to dissolution). WATCH FOR (Week 12 specific risks — read twice) Tonight’s risks center on the temptation to perform closure (rather than honestly close), the dynamics around members not continuing, and the grief that surfaces in any genuine ending. Performed closure. The cohort produces a closing that feels emotionally satisfying but is generic — vague blessings, summary speeches, sentimental moments. The architecture is specific, not generic. The standing pair blessings are particular to what each pair partner has actually witnessed in the other across the formation arc; the body’s Aaronic is shared; the H12.1 card is concrete. Avoidance of grief. A genuine ending produces grief. Th

title: Home
text: danger "YOU ARE VIEWING THE DEV PREVIEW SITE" This is the development preview of the Fellowship of the Heart CCA pilot. Content here is not final and may change without notice. For the published, stable site, visit: jgtittle ministries.github.io/fellowship of the heart pilot at cca Fellowship of the Heart — CCA Pilot (DEV PREVIEW) Three series, walked across a year of Tuesday evenings. The pilot edition of Fellowship of the Heart , the operational expression of the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH) framework, prepared for the first Covenant Christian Academy of Warrenton cohort beginning Fall 2026. The pilot is organized as three sequential series the cohort walks together, with shared participant materials supporting all three: Series Length Focus 1 Getting Started 10 weeks The Four Connects — Self, Others, God, Mission 2 Going Deeper 12 weeks Diagnostic, therapeutic, developmental work of IJH Volume 2 3 Going Out 12 weeks The body sent — discerning and equipping leaders for new groups Read the series in order — each builds on what the cohort has already lived. A cohort that completes only Getting Started has done real work; the deeper series are for those the Spirit prepares to keep walking. Shared participant materials The shared section holds the participant facing artifacts used across all three series: Personal Heart Journal — the daily companion for the whole journey Family Conversation Cards — forty cards organized around the Four Connects Rhythm Card — the daily/weekly/monthly practice card Reading List — annotated guide for parents, teens, and the curious What each series contains Every series follows the same shape: A landing page opening the series and laying out the weeks. A Handbook for the Companion team — the long form context, the theology, the protocols. Weekly Companion Lesson Plans — Quick Reference Card, WATCH FOR, Session at a Glance, Pre Work, Materials, Detailed Run Sheet, Block by Block Scripts, Between session practice, and the IJH source pointer for each session. A CHANGELOG tracking what has shifted between versions. The lesson plans are written for the Companion team. They are detailed on purpose. A new Companion preparing for a heavy session should not be guessing at what to do when the room goes quiet, when a teen weeps, when a parent tries to fix what they should be receiving. The page is a floor under your feet, not a script you read from. Cadence and logistics Series length. Getting Started is 10 weeks; Going Deeper and Going Out are 12 weeks each. With breaks between series, the full pilot runs across a year. Session length. Standard sessions are 90 minutes. Specific commissioning and family night sessions extend to 120 minutes — flagged in each lesson plan. Companion team. A Lead Companion plus a Co Companion team. Most working weeks use a split and merge structure — shared opening and teaching, SPLIT into cohort circles (junior, senior, parent) for the experiential center, MERGE for closing. Participants. Parent–teen pairs, individuals, and households. See each series landing page for the specific fit of that series. Status Curriculum draft, May 2026. Pilot edition for the first Covenant Christian Academy cycle (Fall 2026 onward). These materials will change as the cohort teaches us where they are right, where they bend, and where they break. Each series has its own CHANGELOG. License Released under Creative Commons Attribution ShareAlike 4.0 International . Adapt it for your tradition, walk it with your people, keep the resulting work open in the same spirit. A note on participant content: this license covers the curriculum only. It does not and cannot cover any participant content, journal entries, Discernment Conversation notes, or other cohort material arising from walking the curriculum. Such material remains the property of the participants and is not included in this repository under any license. Contact John G. Tittle, jgtittle ministries. Further up and further in.

title: Family Conversation Cards
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Getting Started series — The Four Connects Family Conversation Cards Forty cards for parents, teens, and the family table Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Pilot edition, May 2026 How to use this deck Forty cards. Ten in each of the four Connects — Self, Others, God, Mission. The cards are not assignments and they are not in order. Pull one when you have ten or fifteen minutes — in the car, at dinner, on a walk, before bed. Three ways to use the deck 1. Pull one card a week Once a week, sit with your teen (or your parent). Pick a card. Read the question aloud. Each person answers, in their own time, in their own way. The card itself does the work — you do not need to add coaching or steer the conversation. Pass on a card if it's the wrong night. 2. Use one when something is in the air When something has been off in your family — quiet at dinner, tension on the way to school, energy gone flat — pull the deck. Find a card that fits. Some are written specifically for moments when something needs naming (cards 14, 17, 22, 27). 3. Run the four Connects across a season Pick four weeks across a quarter. Week one: pull a SELF card. Week two: an OTHERS card. Week three: a GOD card. Week four: a MISSION card. The deck holds the structural sequence; you don't have to. On using cards well The point is the conversation, not the answer. If a card produces five minutes of silence and one honest sentence, that is a complete success. Receive without fixing. When your teen tells you something hard, the temptation is to coach. Resist. Most cards work best when the parent listens twice as much as they talk. Don't push past honest reluctance. “I don't really want to talk about that one tonight” is a complete answer. Try a different card or come back another night. It's okay to draw the same card again later. Some questions deepen on the second or third pass. Most cards have a younger version (“If younger —”) for ages roughly 12–14. Use the version that fits. If you are not safe — please tell someone The work in these pages is private, but you were never meant to carry the heavy things alone. If you are in danger, thinking about hurting yourself, or someone is hurting you, tell a trusted adult today — your Cohort Companion, a parent or guardian, a pastor, or a teacher you trust. Your Cohort Companion: \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ (name and how to reach them) Anytime, day or night: call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline); in an emergency, call 911 . You will not be in trouble for telling. Reaching out is the brave and faithful thing to do — and you will not be alone in it. On the four Connects The deck is organized around the four Connect structure of the IJH Getting Started series: SELF (Cards 1–10) Connecting with yourself. Soil, story, feelings, fears, what you noticed about yourself this week. OTHERS (Cards 11–20) Connecting with each other. Friendship, family, the four conditions of a real container, witnessing, blessing. GOD (Cards 21–30) Connecting with God. Hearing, prayer, doubt, scripture, the garden, presence. MISSION (Cards 31–40) Connecting with mission. Gifts, calling, uphill and downhill, the shadow, what was prepared for you. Printing notes This deck is printed two cards per page (with light color borders to delineate cards). For best use, print on cardstock and cut between cards. Each Connect's cards are color banded by their callout color: blue for Self, green for Others, gold for God, red for Mission. To make the deck physically more durable, you can laminate after cutting. The Cards SELF — Cards 1–10 Card 1 • SELF The first thing you noticed What was the first thing your heart noticed today — before your phone, before breakfast, before anyone else? Just one thing. If younger — What did you wake up thinking about today? Anchor — Psalm 139:1–2 Use it — First thing in the morning, or in the car on the way to school. Card 2 • SELF Soil

title: Shared participant materials
text: Shared participant materials The participant facing artifacts used across all three Fellowship of the Heart series. Unlike the Companion Lesson Plans (which the team holds during a session), these materials live with the participant — in their journal, on the refrigerator, on the dinner table, in their pocket. What's here Personal Heart Journal — the private daily companion for the journey. One participant per copy. The journal scales to what each week actually has time for — a sentence can be a complete entry. Family Conversation Cards — forty cards organized around the Four Connects (Self, Others, God, Mission). Pulled one at a time, used at dinner, in the car, on a walk, when something needs naming. Rhythm Card — the daily / weekly / monthly practice card. The long term rhythm a cohort carries beyond Getting Started, sustained across Going Deeper and Going Out and beyond. PROAPT Card — the six step hearing in scripture practice (Pray, Read, Observe, Apply, Pray again, Tell). Introduced in Getting Started Week 6 and carried daily through the rest of the curriculum. Reading List — an annotated guide for parents, teens, and the curious. Curated from the IJH Volume 5 References for the Fellowship of the Heart audience. How they fit together These five materials are designed to work together across the year: The Personal Heart Journal is the private space where the work of the room continues into the week. The Family Conversation Cards carry the work into the family table. The Rhythm Card sets the daily, weekly, and monthly practices that the cohort sustains together. The PROAPT Card is the reference for the hearing in scripture practice that anchors the daily rhythm from Week 6 onward. The Reading List is for the participant who wants to read further, at their own pace. A cohort can complete Getting Started using only the Personal Heart Journal, the Rhythm Card, and the PROAPT Card; the family cards and reading list are invitations, not assignments. Going Deeper and Going Out lean more heavily on the shared materials as the cohort matures. See also Top level README — the three series overview. Getting Started — where these materials are first introduced. Going Deeper — where the shared rhythm becomes a sustained practice. Going Out — where the materials carry into a sent posture.

title: Interrogating Reality Card
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Going Deeper series Interrogating Reality Card Naming what the cohort already knows but has not yet said Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Pilot edition, draft What this is A corporate knot is something a cohort (or a family, or a church) already senses but has — without ever deciding to — agreed not to say aloud: the gap between what we profess and how we treat each other, the fear no one names, the thing everyone is making up stories about. It is the between us cousin of the individual knots the Going Deeper work already addresses one heart at a time. This card is the practice for naming such a thing safely , locating it, and bringing it to the Lord together. It is the reusable, portable form of what Week 8 — the cohort hears itself does live, and it leans on the same I heard discernment discipline the cohort learned in Week 7 — corporate listening prayer . Anchor scripture. "Buy the truth and do not sell it" (Prov 23:23). The body is one with many members, and each sees in part (1 Cor 12:12–27; 1 Cor 13:9–12). "Speaking the truth in love" (Eph 4:15; cf. Matt 18:15). "Be still, and know" (Ps 46:10). Where two or three are gathered, He is in the midst (Matt 18:20); and the others weigh what is heard (1 Cor 14:29). Why the unsaid does not stay gone What a body will not say, it does not stop knowing — it converts into story. An unspoken reality does not dissolve; people fill the silence with assumption and act on the story rather than the truth. Naming it is how the body returns to what is real, so that the Lord can speak into the real thing. The four guards (do not open it without these) 1. The container and the skill are built first. The deep naming is the last move, never the opening one. The cohort earns it across the weeks of reflective listening and the I heard frame. Never cold. 2. Bounded power in the room. People will not name a thing that indicts those with power over them while those people sit there — the silence is the knot, operating. We do not flatten the room; we submit the conversation to the one Head under whom every member sits (1 Cor 12; Eph 1:22–23). Where that submission is not yet real, go to the standing pair 1:1 route below. 3. Voluntary. No one is required to speak or to disclose. 4. A clean close is possible. Work opened and not closed dissipates rather than integrates. Do not open what the gathering has no time or capacity to close. The movements 0. Build the container and the skill — across Going Deeper (reflective listening; the I heard discipline of Wk 7). 1. Ground truths. Each member names what is real for them ; a partner reflects back what they heard before anyone responds. We are assembling the parts, not winning. 2. Name it, and locate its level. Put the knot plainly on the table, then ask whose it is: this cohort? a pair? those who lead? or how this gathering is being led ? Naming the wrong level mis fires the work. 3. Let silence do the heavy lifting. After it is named, do not rush to fill the silence (Ps 46:10). The Companion holds the container open — the same discipline as Wk 7's silent listen. 4. Bring it to the Lord, not just into the room. The gathered cohort brings the named knot before Him (Matt 18:20) and listens for what He is saying about us, weighing what surfaces (1 Cor 14:29; discernment of voices). This is the Wk 8 move: the body hearing about itself. The Spirit, not the technique, does the work. 5. Commitments, accountability, and a clean close. Each names what they will do, with whom, by when; the cohort may hold one another to it (the Tell). Then close the container deliberately — name what was received, honor those who spoke into risk, release. Fallback — when the room cannot hold it. If the naming produces only silence, or power makes candor unsafe, drop to the standing pair 1:1 : ask each member privately "why the silence?" and "what is the real knot here?" , then resurface to the cohort aggregated

title: Personal Heart Journal
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Getting Started series — The Four Connects Personal Heart Journal A ten week companion for the journey This journal belongs to: \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Pilot edition, May 2026 A Note Before You Begin This journal is a companion for the ten weeks of the Fellowship of the Heart Getting Started series. It is the small, private place where the work each Tuesday continues into your week. Some of what we do in the room only finds its language between sessions — in the quiet of the morning, in the noticing of the day, in a sentence written down at night that surprises you when you read it back. This journal is yours. The team will not collect it. Your cohort will not see it unless you choose. Nothing in it is required. How the journal is set up Each of the ten weeks has its own section. Inside a week you will find: An opening page with the week’s scripture and a brief framing for what we are about to practice. A daily practice page with prompts for each day of the week. Free write space for whatever else surfaces. An end of week reflection. The pages are not assignments. They are scaffolds. If you write a sentence and stop, that is a complete entry. If you fill the margins, that is also a complete entry. The journal scales to what your week actually has time for. Two practices that run every week The morning question (5 min, daily) Before you check your phone in the morning, sit somewhere quiet for five minutes with this question: “Father, what are you up to today — and what do you want me to notice?” Wait sixty seconds. Then write what comes — even if it’s nothing. Even ‘nothing came’ is a complete entry. The pattern recognition develops over weeks, not minutes. The evening journal note (1–2 min, daily) Before bed, write one sentence: “Where did I see God today?” That is the whole practice. One sentence. If you missed Him — write that. Honest noticing is what builds the faculty of seeing. On honesty This journal works only if it is honest. The temptation is to write what sounds spiritual, or what your Companion might approve of, or what your future self will be proud of. Resist that. Write what is actually happening. If your morning question produced nothing, write nothing. If your evening review found you barely thought of God all day, write that. The Spirit meets honest journals; He cannot do much with performances of journals. On returning when you fall away You will miss days. Most people who keep this kind of journal do. When you miss a day, do not start over from the beginning of a streak. Just write today. Returning is the practice; streaks are not. If you miss a whole week, do not go back and fill in the days you missed. Mark them with a single sentence: “This week was hard. I am back today.” That sentence is its own entry. On who reads this No one but you, unless you decide otherwise. Not the team, not your cohort, not your spouse, not your parents. The container the room is built on extends to this journal. Some entries you may want to read aloud to someone trusted — your Cohort Companion, a friend, your weekly partner. That is up to you, on your terms. If you are not safe — please tell someone The work in these pages is private, but you were never meant to carry the heavy things alone. If you are in danger, thinking about hurting yourself, or someone is hurting you, tell a trusted adult today — your Cohort Companion, a parent or guardian, a pastor, or a teacher you trust. Your Cohort Companion: \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ (name and how to reach them) Anytime, day or night: call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline); in an emergency, call 911 . You will not be in trouble for telling. Reaching out is the brave and faithful thing to do — and you will not be alone in it. Begin when you are ready. The first week starts on the ne

title: PROAPT Card
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Getting Started series — The Four Connects PROAPT Card A hearing practice in scripture — six steps, one passage at a time Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Pilot edition, May 2026 What PROAPT is PROAPT is a hearing practice, not a Bible study technique. The distinction matters. A Bible study asks: what does this passage mean? PROAPT asks: what is the Spirit saying through this passage to me, today, in my specific life? Both are valuable. PROAPT is the second. The operational verse for the practice is Romans 10:17 (ESV) : "So faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ." The chain is Word → Hearing → Faith . PROAPT is the simplest practice that works the chain. One short passage a day is enough to start. The six steps The acronym matters because the order matters. P — Pray Invite the Spirit to speak before reading. You are approaching the Word as someone who expects to hear , not just to extract information. Common error: skipping it. "I'll just dive in." R — Read Read the passage. To receive, not to analyze yet. Let it land first. Common error: going straight to interpretation before the passage has had time to land. O — Observe What do you actually notice? What stands out? What is strange or unexpected? Honest observation precedes interpretation. Common error: importing what you already think the passage means. A — Apply What does this mean for you , today, specifically ? This is where the general word (the logos ) becomes a specific word (the rhema ). Common error: applying the passage to other people. P — Pray again Bring the application back to God. Tell Him what you heard, what you are willing to do, what you are struggling to be willing to do. Common error: skipping it. The obedience channel opens here. T — Tell Share what you heard with someone — a partner, your cohort circle, a spouse, a friend. The speaking solidifies the hearing and creates accountability. Common error: skipping it. The most often omitted step. The Tell is what makes the hearing land in the world rather than evaporate by morning. How to use the card Print this page on cardstock. Tuck it into your Bible or your Personal Heart Journal. One short passage at a time. A paragraph from the gospels, a single Psalm, a half chapter of an epistle. PROAPT works best with bite sized passages where one Spirit given specific can actually land. Daily during weeks 6–10 of Getting Started. The Personal Heart Journal has a daily PROAPT template on the Week 6 and Week 7 pages. Sustain into the rhythm afterward. The Rhythm Card lists PROAPT as part of the daily / weekly hearing practice the cohort sustains after Getting Started ends. When the practice goes flat Most participants experience the practice going flat at some point — a week of going through the steps and nothing landing. When that happens: Don't quit. Flat seasons are part of the rhythm. The faculty of hearing develops across years, not weeks. Change the passage type. If you've been in epistles, move to a gospel or a Psalm. If you've been in narrative, sit with a single proverb. Don't skip the Tell. Even if the hearing feels thin, name what you have to a partner. The naming often clarifies what was actually there. Stay short. Reduce to one verse a day for a week. Resist the urge to lengthen the passage to "get something." Bring it to the Companion team. A flat season that lasts more than two weeks is worth surfacing in the cohort circle. The team can hold it with you. IJH source The practice is grounded in IJH Vol 1 Exploration 1 (the Word → Hearing → Faith mechanism) and IJH Vol 2 Seventh Exploration (Hearing with Understanding — the experiential processes by which the hearing faculty develops). The protocol form is A28 Heart Bible Study with PROAPT in IJH Vol 5 Part VI (the Tool Protocols). If you are not safe — please tell someone The work in these pages is private, but you were never meant to carry the heavy thing

title: Reading List
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Getting Started series — The Four Connects Reading List An annotated guide for parents, teens, and the curious Curated from the IJH Volume 5 bibliography John G. Tittle • Pilot edition, May 2026 How to use this list This list is curated for parents, teens, and other family members who are walking the four Connects work — not for academic study. Volume 5 of the IJH project contains a much fuller bibliography for those who want the deeper sources; this list is the on ramp. The books here are organized by Connect, with a small fifth section on integration and rhythm. Each entry has a brief annotation, a tag for who it is for, and a note about when in the journey it is most useful. Not every book is for every reader; pick what fits your season. On reading well Pick one book at a time. Two books in parallel is normal; six books in parallel is a way to read nothing well. Read with a pen. Underline what lands. Write a sentence in the margin when something disagrees with you. Bring what you read to your weekly partner. “Here is a thing I read this week that has stayed with me.” Ten minutes of conversation with one person about a book is worth more than five chapters read alone. Skip what does not land. Return to it in five years. Some books are right for the wrong season; the same book can be exactly right later. If a book unsettles you, that is data. Do not abandon a book just because it is uncomfortable; sometimes the discomfort is the work. But also do not finish a book that is producing harm. On the audience tags Each entry is tagged with one or more of: Audience tags Teen — accessible to senior teens (15–18); some are also for thoughtful junior teens. Parent — written for adult readers; some require some life experience to land. Both — readable across ages; often the books that produce the best parent teen conversations. Advanced — deeper, denser, or theologically heavier; for readers who want the systematic version. Self — the soil, the story, and the wound Books for the work of Connecting with Self — the diagnostic and therapeutic work of understanding your own interior. Wild at Heart: Discovering the Secret of a Man's Soul John Eldredge • 2001 Audience: Teen and Parent (men, primarily). Written for men but read by many women too. Use for: After Week 3 (telling your story) and Week 9 (mission). The book to give the senior teen son or husband whose heart has gone quiet. Eldredge’s central argument — that the masculine heart has design level desires (a battle to fight, an adventure to live, a beauty to rescue) that are wounded by the world and contested by an enemy — is the source material for Vol 2’s Identity Blockage Law. The book is not difficult reading, but it is searching. Many men recognize themselves in it before they can argue about it. Recommended without reservation as a companion to the more systematic IJH framework. Captivating: Unveiling the Mystery of a Woman's Soul John and Stasi Eldredge • 2005 Audience: Teen and Parent (women, primarily). Use for: The companion to Wild at Heart for women. Use after Week 3 or Week 9. Stasi Eldredge’s account of the feminine heart, written with John, working out the design desires (to be romanced, to play an irreplaceable role in a great adventure, to unveil beauty) that mirror the masculine ones. The same caution: the book is searching, and many women recognize the territory before they have argued about it. Waking the Dead: The Glory of a Heart Fully Alive John Eldredge • 2003 Audience: Parent. Some senior teens. Use for: After Wild at Heart. The book that goes deeper into the spiritual warfare dimension of the wound and the false self. Vol 2’s Exploration 2B draws specifically on chapters 9–11 of Waking the Dead for the enemy’s strategy against the heart. The book moves from diagnostic (what is wrong) to therapeutic (the recovery of the heart in Christ).
