title: Rhythm Card
text: FELLOWSHIP OF THE HEART Getting Started series — The Four Connects Daily / Weekly / Monthly Rhythm Card A long term practice for the journey, after Getting Started Based on the Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH), Volumes 1–6 John G. Tittle • Pilot edition, May 2026 Why a rhythm The Getting Started series taught practices. This card is what the practices become — a durable rhythm of life that holds the four Connects in real time across decades, not weeks. From Vol 2 of IJH: a man who has named his interior, has at least one person who knows his story, has the discipline of hearing scripture as the Spirit speaks, and is walking in what was prepared for him is in a different kind of life than a man who is doing none of these. The difference is not effort; it is rhythm. The man who has the rhythm has it because he set it on purpose and has returned to it many times. The man without it does not have the rhythm because nobody helped him build one. This card is the rhythm. Print it. Stick it on your bathroom mirror or your dashboard or the inside of your journal cover. Customize the specifics. Sustain it across the interlude until Going Deeper begins, and then keep it across the seasons that follow. On returning You will fall away from this rhythm. Most of us do, several times a year, sometimes for weeks at a time. When you fall away, do not start over from the beginning. Just begin again on the next available day. Streaks are not the goal. Returning is the goal. The pattern of returning, over years, is what shapes a life. A man who has returned to the morning question fifty times after fifty falls is in a different relationship with God than a man who never started. On scope The rhythm below is the structure. The specifics are yours. A retired grandfather and a high schooler will live this rhythm in different shapes — different times of day, different durations, different weekly partners, different monthly clearings. The structure is universal; the specifics are particular. If a piece of the rhythm does not fit your season — you have a newborn and the morning question is competing with infant feedings, you are in finals and the monthly clearing time is impossible — do not abandon the whole rhythm. Keep what is doable; return to the rest when the season changes. Daily Five to fifteen minutes a day. Two practices that take five minutes each. One optional practice that takes ten to fifteen minutes. The morning question (5 min) Practice Before you check your phone, sit somewhere quiet for five minutes with this question: “Father, what are you up to today — and what do you want me to notice?” Wait sixty seconds. Then write what comes — even if it’s nothing. Even ‘nothing came’ is a complete entry. The pattern recognition develops over weeks, not minutes. Why the morning Because the day has not yet shaped you. Because your phone has not yet shaped you. Because you are at your most receptive in the first ten minutes after waking, and the question gets to occupy the most impressionable real estate of your day. If your morning is genuinely impossible — small children, early commute, night shift schedule — pick another moment that has the same quality. The first quiet ten minutes after the rush has settled. The walk into the office. The first cup of coffee at the desk. Not the literal morning, but the first available pre formed quiet of the day. Common variations Two minute version, when five is impossible. The question, sixty seconds of waiting, one written sentence. That is enough to keep the practice alive. Spoken version, in the car on the way to work or school. Same question. Listen as you drive. Speak the answer aloud at a stoplight if one comes. Walking version. Take the question out the door and onto a five minute walk. Watch what surfaces. The evening journal note (1–2 min) Practice Before bed, write one sentence: “Where did I see God today?” That is the whole practice. One sentence. If you missed Him — write that. Honest noticin

title: Start here
text: Start here — for the curious You are at the front door of Fellowship of the Heart . This page is the short answer to: what is this, and how is walking it supposed to bring a person closer to God? What this is Fellowship of the Heart (FotH) is what it looks like when a real cohort walks the work of the Intentional Journey of the Heart together. Three sequential series, across about a year of Tuesday evenings: Getting Started — ten weeks introducing the four Connects: Self → Others → God → Mission. Going Deeper — twelve weeks of the harder interior work, and learning to hear God as a body. Going Out — twelve weeks of the body sent: into household, vocational, third place contexts, and sometimes into starting new formation groups. Plus the shared participant materials — the Personal Heart Journal, the Family Conversation Cards, the Rhythm Card, the Reading List — that walk alongside all three series. The Intentional Journey of the Heart (IJH) sits behind FotH. IJH is the six volume record of how God's love actually works in a human heart — the operative laws, the diagnostics for where you are, the practices that move you forward. FotH is what those volumes look like when a cohort actually walks them. IJH is the what and the why ; FotH is the how and the with whom . The six volumes: Volume 1: Laws of the Spirit — the 46 Foundational Laws (38 of wide consent — cause and effect relationships affirmed across mainstream Christian tradition — plus eight newer ones still being tested), and eight personal Explorations of less mapped territory. The structural and operational laws underneath the spiritual life. Volume 2: Knowing to Doing — the diagnostic, therapeutic, and developmental work — the Heart Soil, the Emotional Knots, the Container, the Tool Map, PROAPT, the Four Connects, the Training Plan. FotH is the cohort walked operational form of Vol 2 most directly. Volume 3: Spiritual Dynamics — the most speculative of the volumes; attempts to translate Vol 1's structural laws into more formal mathematical and dynamical language. Skippable on a first pass unless you enjoy that frame. Volume 4: Testing Framework — how to test all of this in real life, at individual, small group, and larger study scales. Volume 5: References — annotated bibliography, the Periodic Table of Spiritual Laws (the catalog organizer), the Formation Documents (the academic register companion papers — including MSFIG and the four part Hearing God Papers , which measure spiritual maturity in hearing God, prescribe its cultivation, and form the heart through the lower and upper levels of internalization), and the operational tool protocols Companions deploy in sessions. Volume 6: Governance — the framework under which the work is stewarded, extended, and corrected by others beyond the original author. The institutional layer that helps the work outlast its author. Every claim in IJH carries one of three confidence tiers, named explicitly so you can read with the right posture: Clearly Taught — multi author, multi Testament scriptural support; broadly accepted across mainstream Christian traditions. High confidence claims you can operate in. Reasonably Inferred — derivable from scripture by sound reasoning and consistent with experience, but not stated explicitly or widely enough to clear the top tier. Held loosely; needs community testing. Speculative — interesting enough to pursue, not yet solid enough to build on. The IJH site is at jgtittle ministries.github.io/Intentional Journey of the Heart; the Read Me First is the substantive front door. How walking this brings you closer to God The work rests on a claim about how formation actually happens. Most people experience their interior life as noise — feelings, impulses, half formed prayers, things that should be there but aren't.
